title: Acoustic detector clock alignment and quality gate

primary_sources.relevance: AudioContext.getOutputTimestamp maps audio context time to the browser performance timeline; the specification warns that currentTime minus contextTime is not a reliable latency estimate.
Performance timing uses a stable monotonic clock within a browser execution, while cross-device or cross-execution timestamps require explicit alignment and remain subject to resolution and jitter.

finding: A detector trace cannot be scored against browser-scheduled pulse times until the detector clock is mapped into the browser performance timeline. Bracketing synchronization anchors support an affine offset-and-drift estimate; residual and detector resolution must be retained as uncertainty and gated before attribution scoring.

artifact.method: median pairwise affine clock estimate with residual and drift quality gate

cases: recovers offset and small drift from bracketing anchors
limits one noisy anchor with a robust median estimator
retains original detector time and alignment uncertainty

uncertainty: The estimator assumes approximately affine clock drift during a short challenge window.
Synchronization anchors must themselves be observed on both timelines; poor anchors can create false confidence.
Clock calibration does not establish speaker emission, microphone placement, or human perception.
falsification_route: Generate fresh anchors before and after each acoustic challenge. Reject synchronized attribution if residual or drift thresholds fail, or if aligned detections no longer match the randomized challenge under uncertainty-expanded timing bounds.
privacy_boundary: No microphone recording, private conversation, health information, credential, or identifying device data is stored in this evidence packet.
